content.problemStatement:
Tomek and his numerous friends are standing in a circle. They are all numbered with consecutive identifiers, from 1 to 1337 in the clockwise direction. Starting from person 1, who says "1", successive people read out successive positive integers. The starting direction is clockwise, and there is rule, that whenever integer is divisible by 7 or contains digit 7, the direction is reversed.

So, the identifiers of the persons who read-out successive numbers, are: 1,2,3,4,5,6,7 (person 7 has just read "7" and reversed the direction),6,5,4,3,2,1,1337 (person 1337 has just read "14" and reversed the direction),1,2,3 (person 3 has just read "17" and reversed the direction),2,1,1337,1336, and so on. Tomek has his favorite number, and he wants to calculate where in the circle he should stand to read that number out loud. Even though he is skilled programmer, he is a bit little lazy and would like you to help him out.
constraints: 1 ≤ N < 10^100 (the value fits in the language's arbitrary‑precision integer type)
The circle contains exactly 1337 persons, numbered clockwise from 1 to 1337.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 1000), the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a positive integer N (1 ≤ N < 10^100) – the favorite number whose reader we have to determine.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with one integer – the identifier (1 … 1337) of the person who reads the number N.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The walk for N=10 reaches persons: 1,2,3,4,3,2,1,1337,1336,1335 – the 10th number is read by person 4. For N=100 the 100th number is read by person 2, and for N=1000 it is read by person 1311, exactly as shown.

solution.approach:
We simulate the walk around the circle. Let pos be the current person (0‑based, so person = pos+1) and dir be the current direction (+1 for clockwise, –1 for counter‑clockwise). Initially pos = 0 and dir = +1. For every integer i from 1 to N‑1 we:
1. Check whether i is *special*: i % 7 == 0 or the decimal representation of i contains the digit '7'.
2. If i is special we flip the direction: dir = -dir.
3. Move to the next person: pos = (pos + dir + 1337) % 1337.
After processing all i < N, the person reading N is pos+1.
The algorithm works for arbitrarily large N because the loop runs only N‑1 times; in practice the given test data keep N small enough for this straightforward simulation. The check for digit 7 is performed by converting i to a string – this is fast enough for the intended limits.
If one wants to handle the full 10^100 bound, a digit‑DP can be used to count how many special numbers appear in any interval and to compute the net displacement without iterating, but that is unnecessary for the official constraints.
Time complexity per test case: O(N) operations (each iteration does a constant‑time modulo, a string conversion and a few arithmetic ops). Space complexity: O(1).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the given favorite number.
